answer: Minh Nguyen works on statistical methods and observables for extracting cosmological information from astronomical observations, with interests including field-level inference, EFT-based cosmological modeling, and redshift-space statistics.

Field-level inference aims to extract cosmological information directly from high-dimensional observed fields, rather than compressing the data only into summary statistics such as the power spectrum. Minh is interested in applying these methods to large-scale structure and redshift-space analyses.

questions: What is the effective field theory of large-scale structure?
answer: Minh is interested in EFT-based cosmological modeling as a way to connect theoretical predictions, simulations, and observational statistics for large-scale structure.

questions: Do you accept visitors or postdocs?
answer: For collaboration inquiries, please use the contact information listed on this homepage. Relevant topics include field-level inference, cosmological statistics, large-scale structure, and EFT-based modeling.
Please use the contact information listed on this homepage.

Minh is currently a Kavli IPMU Fellow at the Kavli Institute for the Physics and Mathematics of the Universe, University of Tokyo. Previously, he was a Leinweber Fellow at the Leinweber Center for Theoretical Physics, University of Michigan, and completed his PhD at the Max Planck Institute for Astrophysics and LMU Munich.

questions: What is your work on the growth of structure?
Tell me about the suppressed growth result
What is the S8 tension work?
answer: Minh contributed to the first ~4-sigma evidence for a late-time suppression in the growth of large-scale structure. The result was published in Physical Review Letters (2023) and covered by Scientific American and New Scientist.
Minh received the 2024 Buchalter Cosmology Prize for work on field-level inference.

questions: Tell me about your public engagement
answer: Minh organizes the VLLT Joint Seminar Series for Vietnamese undergraduate and graduate students in physics and astronomy. He is also a Science Communication Fellow at the University of Michigan Museum of Natural History and participates in Cosmology from Home Ask-Me-Anything sessions.
